# Code Review — 2026-06-16

## Scope
- CLI entry point (`cmd/symfetch/main.go`)
- Core fetch layer (`internal/fetch/`)
- MCP server (`internal/mcp/`)
- Pipeline orchestration (`internal/pipeline/`)
- Cache system (`internal/cache/`)
- Batch processing (`internal/batch/`)
- Configuration (`internal/config/`)
- CI workflows (`.github/workflows/`)

## Evidence base
- Full source read of all production packages
- CI workflow review
- No open issues or PRs; repo at rest

---

## Findings

### UX/DX

- [ ] **[UX/DX] Multi-URL CLI silently swallows partial failures**
  - **Status quo:** `runRaw` and the main loop in `newRootCmd` print errors to stderr but always return `nil`. Scripts calling `symfetch url1 url2 url3` cannot detect that 1-2 URLs failed. See `cmd/symfetch/main.go:155-173` and `cmd/symfetch/main.go:201-220`.
  - **Proposed solution:** Track whether any fetch failed and return a non-nil error (or a dedicated exit code) when at least one URL failed. Alternatively, add a `--fail-fast` flag that aborts on first error and a `--fail-on-any` flag that returns non-zero if any URL failed.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / high impact — scripts and CI pipelines using symfetch currently have no way to detect partial failures.

- [ ] **[UX/DX] MCP timeout cap is undocumented and inconsistent with CLI**
  - **Status quo:** MCP `fetch_url` caps `timeout_seconds` at 120s (`internal/mcp/tools.go:20`), but the CLI `--timeout` flag has no maximum — a user can pass `--timeout 999s` without warning. The MCP cap is only visible in source, not in tool description or README.
  - **Proposed solution:** Document the MCP timeout cap in the tool description schema. Consider adding a CLI-side warning or cap for extremely large timeout values, or at minimum document the difference.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact — prevents confusion when switching between CLI and MCP usage.

### Architecture

- [ ] **[Architecture] Duplicated markdown metadata formatting between CLI and MCP**
  - **Status quo:** `printMarkdownResult` in `cmd/symfetch/main.go:272-284` and `formatWithMeta` in `internal/mcp/tools.go:198-214` contain near-identical logic for prepending title/status/token metadata to markdown output. Changes to the metadata format must be applied in both places.
  - **Proposed solution:** Extract a shared `render.FormatMarkdownWithMeta(res *pipeline.Result) string` function in `internal/render/` or `internal/pipeline/`. Both callers import and use it.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / medium impact — eliminates a maintenance divergence point.

- [ ] **[Architecture] Pipeline creates a new Cache instance per Run call**
  - **Status quo:** `pipeline.Run` instantiates `cache.New(dir, ttl)` on every call when caching is enabled (`internal/pipeline/pipeline.go:88-115`). In batch mode (`batch.Pool.RunBatch`), this means N concurrent cache instances for N URLs, each loading the index file independently. The index file uses atomic write+rename but concurrent loads can cause redundant I/O.
  - **Proposed solution:** Accept an optional `*cache.Cache` in `pipeline.Options` (or a `CacheFactory` func) so the batch pool can share a single cache instance across workers. Fall back to per-call creation when nil.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Medium effort / medium impact — reduces redundant index loads in batch mode and centralizes cache lifecycle.

### Security

- [ ] **[Security] CLI `--allow-private` bypasses SSRF guard without audit trail**
  - **Status quo:** The `--allow-private` flag (`cmd/symfetch/main.go:191`) disables the SSRF guard for all URLs in the command. There is no log message or warning when this flag is used, making it invisible in shared environments or CI logs. The MCP server correctly hardcodes `AllowPrivate: false` (`internal/mcp/tools.go:106`).
  - **Proposed solution:** When `--allow-private` is active, emit a one-line warning to stderr: `"warning: SSRF guard disabled — fetching private/loopback addresses is permitted"`. This creates an audit trail without blocking the feature.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / high impact — security-relevant behavior should be visible in logs, especially in automated environments.

### Performance

- [ ] **[Performance] Cache eviction scans directory on every Put when over size**
  - **Status quo:** `evictIfOverSize` in `internal/cache/cache.go:166-194` is called after every `Put`. When the cache is near or over `maxSize` (100MB), it sorts all entries and walks the index. For write-heavy workloads (batch fetches of 20 URLs), this O(n log n) sort runs 20 times in rapid succession.
  - **Proposed solution:** Debounce eviction: only run `evictIfOverSize` if the last eviction was more than N seconds ago (e.g. 30s), or track cumulative added size and only evict when it exceeds a threshold since last eviction.
  - **Effort/Impact:** Low effort / low impact — only affects batch workloads hitting the size limit, but easy to fix.

---

## Conclusion

The codebase is well-structured with solid security fundamentals (SSRF guard, atomic cache writes, 0700 cache dirs). The highest-impact quick win is **fixing silent partial failures in multi-URL CLI mode** — this directly affects scriptability and is a one-line return-value change. The second priority is **extracting the duplicated markdown metadata formatter** to prevent drift between CLI and MCP output.

Recommended first quick win: `cmd/symfetch/main.go` — track fetch errors and return non-zero on partial failure.
